title: How Erosion Shapes Landscapes Over Time

imageCredit.creator: Rachel from Los Angeles, California, USA

lesson.learn:
Erosion reshapes landscapes by removing rock and soil through water, wind, ice, gravity, and chemical weathering. It does not build from scratch so much as it carves away what is already there. That carving can create arches, canyons, hoodoos, valleys, and river terraces depending on the rock type and climate.

The surprising part is that erosion is creative. By taking material away, it reveals structure, layers, and form that were hidden before.
lesson.simpler: Erosion changes landscapes by wearing away rock and soil with water, wind, ice, and gravity.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Landscapes are sculpted by removal as much as by building.
lesson.deeperDive:
Different agents of erosion leave different signatures. Rivers cut channels and canyons. Ice carves broad U-shaped valleys. Wind can sandblast exposed rock into arches and fins. Rainwater dissolves some rocks chemically, especially limestone. In each case, the pattern depends on rock hardness, climate, uplift rate, and time.

That is why erosion is one of geology's most visible processes. It works slowly, but it leaves dramatic results.
lesson.funFact: Erosion is why some of Earth's most famous rock shapes exist.
references: National Park Service - Arches and Bryce Canyon geology

people: Park geologists - interpreters of landscape change
places: River valleys - carved by flowing water
Deserts - where wind erosion is strong
Mountain slopes - where gravity and water move material
years: 1800s - erosion becomes a core geology concept
Present day - erosion is tracked globally
concepts: Weathering - breaking down rock in place
Arches - rock windows carved by erosion
Landscape evolution - long-term change in landforms
